speaker: interviewer
text: Los Angeles, April 4, 1995. It's tape six.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six.

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In this tape, you have to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And to make sure. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Three.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I returned to my friends and told them, she doesn't want to hide us. In that point, we didn't know what to do. But nearby the-- the-- we did notice a single farm-- a type of farm-- we-- we did-- we didn't go into villages, but single farms we did go in for-- for food or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to-- in over there. And to my surprise, I noticed that the farmer-- farmer's daughter was my schoolmate. And so everything warmed up, and they did give us food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked him. Listen, maybe that he would hide us for money. And we took out a lot of gold, put it on the table. And his daughter did like the earrings. And his wife did like the ring. And we told her, you can take it. You can keep it. And he told us, go back to the forest. Come tomorrow, and I will think it over. Anyway, we wind out being in his place. He built a hiding place in the barn. He put a table in the corner in the barn. He covered the table with straw. The entrance was from outside. He removed a panel. And we bent down. And we did crawl to the stable. It would be a long story to describe everything-- how life over there. One day--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in this hiding place October, November, December, January, February, March. About a 1/2 a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till March '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: April.

speaker: interviewer
text: April.

speaker: interviewee
text: To April.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- to tell you about how life over there, I, again, could stay a whole night. So I will just tell you that-- that he-- the agreement was he was going to take food to us in the evening for a-- to walk. He should give us three-- three times food. He didn't do the things. But we understood. He did give us only one meal. So it was OK with us. We didn't have a grudge against him. We understood. If he want to be careful, it's only better for us. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --but he had something in his mind that he hurt us, because when there were rumors that the Russians had come in, he did come into hiding place. Oh, I'm afraid. I didn't give-- give us much food, and so on. So he will take when he come back-- back from me, whatever you've given me. And we told him, Mr. Bojarski. I have a house in Izbica. I will sign it to you. I understood you are afraid. And his mind was that. Besides this, he-- he did probably want all our money, because one evening, he said, listen guys. We-- I know the Germans are now searching all the houses close to the forest. I'm afraid they will come to my place. And they are now not far away-- about a mile and a 1/2. I prepared for you a new hiding place. And be whatever, about three days till the Germans will come through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought our hiding place will be far away in the-- in the forest someplace. He took us behind the barn. It was dark. It was a little roof, I remember. I didn't know where I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I notice in the moonlight, I noticed a carriage with two wheels. On top of the carriage was a millstone. It was standing in the side. And now he took me by mine-- you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Waist?

speaker: interviewee
text: --waist, and he pushed me in the hole. And then later, another guy, and the third guy. And later, we ask him for a-- for a kerosene lamps. So he could give us a lamp. And I look around. And we're in a-- like in a grave. It was about, I'd say, about five-- four feet wide-- four feet square. We thought, three days. Not a-- now I can't imagine being two hours. Three days is not a long time. And there was on top, I remember it was a two by four with some branches. I don't know what he has on top. Anyway, there was a hole in the side, and he-- where he pushed it in. He pushed in straw now. I've seen it now with a kerosene lamp-- pushed in the straw. And we're happy. Now I hear some-- he-- something is-- some wheels turning on top over the roof of our hiding place. And now I hear a bam! Something fall down on top of the roof. And the two by four cracked like a V. I-- I-- now, the lamp start to flicker, and it's out. And I take the matches and burn it. It burns, flickers out. And lighted the match-- didn't want to burn. And finally, we feel we have no air. So I said, we must go to the farmer, tell him that the roof did break. He tried to push up the straw. And our one guy did hold with his arm the-- the two by four support-- he shouldn't bring it down completely. And the straw doesn't want to go up. So he pushed down the straw. And he said-- Kostman, it was-- not Kostman. He-- yeah, Kostman-- sorry. He said, something is blocking the entrance-- the-- the opening. And we don't have air. And finally, he was able to move it a little bit, and fresh air did come in. And fresh air did come in. Finally, he pushed his hand, and this did slide, the-- whatever was on top. We went out. He went out. He called the farmer. The farmer did come back and said, oh. You must stay. You must go back to the old place. What will happen will happen. When he-- we went out, and he did come with the lamp. What I have seen is this. This two wheel carriage was in the beginning of this hiding place on the top. But-- and-- and the handles were high, and the stone was laying on the roof. What he has done is if he would do it slowly, probably, the stone would block to the-- the opening, and we would be suffocated. But when he picked up the handle, the stone fall down. And probably the stone did break the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Two by four.

speaker: interviewee
text: --two by four. Otherwise, Kostman wouldn't be able to move the-- the heavy stone. But because it was a angle-- [LAUGHS] --it did slide. So this way-- this way we went back to the old place, and-- through the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you felt that he had tried to kill you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But again, hope-- maybe it's a mistake. Maybe it's not like that. We had, inside, we had a feeling that he did try-- but still. Two days later, we are waiting for food. Seven o'clock, there's no food. So maybe something happened. He can't. And now again, this I even missed in my book. I don't write about this, because it's not too believable. Usually we slept-- like imagine this table-- two next to each other. The third one, every day-- every-- somebody else has duty-- how to say-- to pick up the food, to give up the waste. We had two pots. One pot would come with food, and another pot we did give the farmer with waste. So-- so the guy who had duty was sleeping close to the opening. And that time was my duty. I was in close. But then seven o'clock, eight o'clock, food doesn't come. So I said, hell, it's cold there. We will sleep together. So Kostman said, I don't want to be so crowded. And he went to my place. But nine o'clock, we heard footsteps. The farmer always-- when he'd open the opening, the panel-- he did go around so nobody sighting. And we recognized his steps. You know, so many-- and we are happy he did bring us food. And he is preparing Kostman to pick up the food, to under the table. And we had the panels opened. And he slide out. And soon, we see a flash of fire and a bam from a gun. And Kostman fall back, wounded. And he start off-- eeh-- scurr-- listening to him-- it's like people-- dead people. I don't know. But he-- he died. He did suffer a little bit-- it took maybe a minute. And now the panel is closed. And we alone. And we realize something terrible happened. Anyway, we were afraid, probably. He planned it. We are sure that one will come out easy for the food. It will be easy to kill. Now we had two desperate people. We were afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they removed the straw from the stratum of the straw, from the table. And at that time, I moved in one side. It was a little bit straw on one side, my friend firstly, I, the second. Anyway, they remove the-- uncovered the table. They were looking for me, for us, and discovered me. And he was standing with a gun. Where's the first one?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I told him, he's killed. I begged him not to shoot at to me. Nevertheless, he shot at me. And I pretended to be dead immediately. How fast the brain could work. I make like that. Ah. I felt-- I felt here a hard punch-- that's all. The bullet is still here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still have the bullet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You could feel it, even if you give me your hand. And I pretend to be dead. And later, because I blocked the way to the third one, they put in-- they made a lasso. They took a belt, and they tied my legs, and they pulled me out and put me in the-- it was raining in that day. And they neglected me. And I was thinking maybe now to escape. I didn't. And they shot at another one. And later, I did-- they pulled me back, thinking that I am dead. There's more to it, because Bojarski, the farmer, suspect that I'm alive. And I was aware that people die-- they are-- they had-- they are stiff. So I made myself stiff. I didn't know when. Probably not immediately they get stiff. But he didn't know probably either. I made them stiff-- made-- attached my hand. When Bojarski said, maybe we shoot him. We should give him another bullet. So he-- another guy touched my hand. It was dark, but I felt. And he said, oh, it's stiff already. And so anyway, they pushed me back. And he said-- I heard the voices-- now let's collect the money, and we will put them-- we will dig it. We put them in grave tomorrow. And they left. At that point, I did get up and escaped. There was a old brick factory. And I was hiding in the-- I went to-- it was an [NON-ENGLISH] center-- brick factory. And over there, with the help of my teachers, Polish teachers, they-- they did give me food. And later, when they were chasing for me-- because always, they were looking for me. Because they were aware, if I survived, they'd be in trouble. I managed to go to another village. Over there, I attached myself to a Polish underground organization. [NON-ENGLISH]-- this was a peasant partisan organization. They didn't know I'm Jewish. And I was over there till the liberation. After the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians, in 1944, in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you stayed in Izbica afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was-- I was-- a Polish lady told me-- she was a friend of us, and she was-- she owns a bakery. And she told me, [NON-ENGLISH], don't be all like another Jews-- just business, business, [NON-ENGLISH]. Go to a decent work. Be a baker. I didn't know much. I was 12 years old. And my whole center of the world seemed to be to be Izbica.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even now.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now you were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I was 16-- 15, 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: 16. So anyway, I start to work in a bakery. But she told me, don't go out to the streets. And I-- I was wondering. I'm-- I'm liberated. Why shouldn't I? And once, they knocked on the door, and she hid me-- some people. And later, they left. In a fork over there, Perchesky told me, Tom, don't stay in Izbica. Run. Because you'll be in trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next day, I did go out to the town, and I did thought, I will go to a bus and take me to Lublin. There were no buses. And the-- and the Russians didn't stop in this little place. They were afraid, because there were still partisans and so on, and so on-- Polish nationalists. And they just passed by.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, I notice three guys after me. Wherever I move, they going. And one of them was the guy who shot at me. So I realize, God, they were chasing me for so long. They didn't get me. And now-- and I could see the smile on their faces that-- like saying, now we have you. And when I was trying to stop the bus-- the trucks, they didn't stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought, it start to be dark. It will be dark. I am shot for sure. So I don't care. Let the truck run me over. When I've seen the next truck going down the mountain-- the hill, I jumped on the road. And it-- it stopped maybe two steps before me. The truck stopped. And the Russian officer did run out-- start to scream.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since I told him, listen, I'm dead anyway. Take me or run me over. He took me. And I left. And I still remember-- and he-- there wasn't place inside, so I was in the back. And there was a anti-aircraft gun. I was sitting-- [LAUGHS] --on the seat of that aircraft gun looking down. And I'm seeing the faces of the guys. And he took me to Lublin this way. And later, this was liberation. We was in Lublin. And later, it was the Polish army. And later, I was working as social director in 1957 where-- where Jews were able to leave Poland. I applied for a visa to Israel, left for Israel. In Israel, I met my American wife. She was a tourist. I met her. Six months later, we married. And in 1959, she took me to the States. I was working in the beginning as a janitor till I learned the language. And later, I opened a electronic business in Santa Barbara. And for some 20 years, I opened-- I owned a business. And I feel all of a sudden, oh, I must finish my book. It's important.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I quit my business 10 years ago and was working on my two books, which I finished. And one is accepted and will be out in spring next year. By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Northwestern University Press. I felt also that I must do something important. The Poles put in a sign in Sobibor that here were killed 250,000 Russians, Poles, Jews, gypsies. And it was wrong. And they built that church over there. In the place where they killed people, they built a kindergarten with swings and carousels, everything. And all this bothered me-- and told lies that Polish children from Lublin were killed over there. And I go every year to Poland and went to Sobibor. And when I've seen such a guide telling false stories, I interrupted, and told the true story. I was a pest. And it took me years and years. And I established an organization, Holocaust Site Preservation Committee, with very famous people-- senators, and politicians, and artists. And with their help, I managed finally last year to change, to take down the sign and put in a truthful sign that here were killed 250,000 Jews, in plain language. And this, I accomplished last year. And I think this is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've made a lot of videos. And I particularly remember your one video when you went back and you bent down--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. 'Past and Present', yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you held the piece of bone, of human bone. You've done a great deal to-- to put Sobibor into the consciousness of humanity.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't tell you. I met this Nazi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was called to Germany, because this Nazi, he'd received seven life sentences for him personally killing 30 people and being accessory in killing 40 million people. But the Germans, if you didn't kill by yourself, you just pushed the people to the gas chambers. You're only an accessory. After being 6 and 1/2-- 16 and a 1/2 years in jail, he was released, because his lawyer did win a retrial for some technical reasons. He received back the-- the life-- the life in jail sentence. But while I was over there, and I was in process of writing my book, I felt I should talk to him. He did live in a hotel in Hagen. And he went in the morning to the court and returned to the hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ask a friend of mine to go and ask him some questions, because I need it for my book. My friend, a German journalist, he left. And later, 15 minutes later, I have a telephone. Tom, come. He want to talk to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was shocked. My knees start to shake. I'm not a scared guy, but from the emotion. And I didn't know what to do. Go to him is making him again a person. Not to go, I will lose some important information. I have witnesses. I have this. But this will be the first time when I talk-- Nazi talks to his victims. It's important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- but I was afraid that my friends would point a finger. How could you? And this is true. They point a finger at me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I said, I don't care. I will be gone. My friends will be gone. But what is written down will be for history. And I said, I'm going. And I went. And I remember I didn't want to meet him in the home. I didn't want to-- we agreed I will meet him in the lobby. I open the door to the lobby, come in. He was sitting in a table. And I remember when he sees me, he stood up like a German officer should. I think he even tried to reach my hand. Of course, I didn't shake him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- so my first question was, do you remember me? His answer was-- I still can't understood-- he say, like it would be yes or no. He said, no. You were such a little boy. So I don't know. We talked for four hours. And I tell you something. I-- people ask me if I was afraid. I wasn't afraid. Why could I be afraid? He's a old man, you know? But I now I think I was afraid, because it's not true. That as a matter of fact, my wife was standing by, and I asked my wife. And my wife said, Tom, if I wouldn't know you and wouldn't know him, and if people would ask, would tell me one is a murderer, one is a victim, who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, I would point at you. You have more-- your face-- you-- you look more tough here-- round face, like a nice grandfather, pinkish. That's what I was afraid. Because it's not true that the murderers were deranged people, sick people. No. They were perfectly normal people, and he was a example of it. How easy, how very delicate, how very fragile the crust of our civilization is, our culture. How given the right-- have the right circumstances, it could crack and the beast would come out. And that what I was afraid, and still I am afraid. I could show you his picture, with this time, my cousin make-- me with him together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frenzel-- Karl Frenzel. He received-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now I want to ask you a question. If-- if you were-- what would you say to people now and in the future, maybe hundreds of years from now, who say that Jews did not revolt, they did not show courage, they went like sheep to the slaughter, they didn't-- they didn't fight back. What would you say to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You could fight back if you have hope. You see, there was no hope. And we were demoralized. Everybody did hunted us. A lot of us were killed in the beginning. The families were killed. So the lust for life was lost. Nevertheless, we fought. We fought more than a lot of nations. There were five million Russian prisoners of war were killed in the camps. There were young people, trained in the world war, and they didn't resist. And here, the miserable Jews did resist. So this is the reason why Sobibor is neglected, still the reason. Because you see, if you will go now to Poland, and you go to a tourist office, they should take you to the places, of-- to concentration places. They will take you to Majdanek. They will omit Sobibor. Because going to Sobibor, they must tell the story of Sobibor. They must tell the story of the revolt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the question is, yes. There were another camps. Why didn't the Poles revolted? So they must tell about the Jews. The question is, did the Poles revolted, too? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were also over three million Poles killed. And so Jews did revolt, but unfortunately, too late, too little. But considering-- taking all the circumstances, it's to judge the history of the Jews in time of the war, you must know the full history, not even the time at all-- going back-- and the conditions we used to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: For example, I talked with Frenzel, and I ask him, so what is your opinion about the revolt? Oh, he said, when I read the papers about Israel, how they fight for their country, and what kind of brave soldiers they were, I wonder why you didn't do that over there? Why so late?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't have-- I mean, I didn't even go in to explain him, the conditions were, the ghettos, the suffering, the killings. So I could talk a long time. Unfortunately, you must finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tom, I want to thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. Yeah. It's a lot to talk about.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's a lot to talk about.

speaker: interviewee
text: You imagine how it's hurt when they say it never happened now, on that people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you know, I was within miles of you everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Lvov, Stryi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stryi?

speaker: interviewer
text: Zaklikow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah,

speaker: interviewer
text: All those places.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. The same places.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the same forests.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I know the stories you've told, and I thank you from the bottom of my heart--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for explaining it so clearly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, OK.
